one_line_takeaway: A taxonomy-and-formalization survey that unifies adversarial attacks on deep-RL agents as perturbations of a POMDP's observation function O or transition function T, and organizes adversarial-training defenses (constant, co-trained, mixture, magnitude/frequency/regularization control) into a single DRO-based min-max framework.
key_finding: The paper's main takeaway is organizational rather than empirical: adversarial attacks on RL can be unified as perturbations to two POMDP components (observation function O and transition function T), and the same worst-case-adversary machinery that attacks agents is the most widely used and most flexible route to robustifying them via adversarial training. It surfaces recurring practical lessons drawn from the surveyed literature: constant single adversaries cause the protagonist to over-fit / 'reward-hack' a narrow threat model and stay brittle to stronger or unseen attacks; co-training is more effective but non-stationary and unstable (typically requiring more adversary updates than protagonist updates, N_xi > N_pi); mixtures/populations of adversaries and worst-episode selection reduce robust over-fitting; perturbation magnitude must be tuned (too small = no robustness gain, too large = task unsolvable); and there is an intrinsic robustness-vs-clean-performance trade-off. It also flags open problems: training stability (borrowing from GAN/MARL Nash-equilibrium techniques), explainability, human-in-the-loop/RLHF, and the emerging two-way interaction between LLMs and adversarial RL.
core_contribution: This is a survey (not an empirical paper) whose OWN contributions are threefold and should not be confused with the many prior methods it catalogs. (1) A unifying formalization of adversarial robustness for deep RL: it models the agent's world as a POMDP Omega=(S,A,T,R,X,O), casts robustness as distributionally-robust optimization over an uncertainty set of environment alterations (max_pi min over perturbation distributions of expected return, Eq. 14), and introduces an explicit 'adversary-augmented transition' construction in which an attacker agent xi acts jointly with the protagonist pi inside a single combined POMDP (Eq. 15-16). (2) A new taxonomy of adversarial attacks on RL organized along orthogonal axes: the perturbed element (observation x_t, action a_t, current state s_t before transition, the transition function T itself, or next state s_{t+1} after transition), the altered POMDP component (observation function O vs. transition/dynamics function T), attacker knowledge (white-box gradient attacks vs. black-box derivative-free / adversarial-policy attacks), and objective (short-term divergence vs. long-term adversarial reward). A key conceptual device introduced by the survey is the distinction between 'perturb' (modifying an element of a transition tuple) and 'alter' (the resulting change to a POMDP component). (3) An organized review that maps existing attacks and adversarial-training strategies onto this taxonomy, plus a computational-cost analysis of attack families and a catalog of tooling. The survey deliberately scopes itself to observation alterations (phi_O) and dynamics alterations (phi_T), explicitly excluding state-set, action-set, observation-support, and reward alterations (phi_S, phi_A, phi_X, phi_R) as out-of-scope.
threat_model: An adversary xi degrades an RL agent's cumulative reward by injecting bounded perturbations either into the agent's observations (an alteration of the observation function O -- e.g., falsified sensor readings on an autonomous vehicle, imperceptible pixel perturbations) or into the environment dynamics (an alteration of the transition function T -- perturbing the current state before transition, the transition rule, the next state, or the agent's executed action). Attacker knowledge ranges from white-box (gradient access to the policy, enabling FGSM/PGD/C&W-style attacks) to black-box (query-only derivative-free attacks such as NES/SPSA/SimBA/Square/HSJA, or trained adversarial policies such as RARL/ATLA/PR-MDP that need no gradients at runtime). Perturbations are budget-constrained via Lp norms (L0/L1/L2/Linf) or distributional divergences (KL, Wasserstein). Crucially, the survey frames the SAME adversary constructively: the min-max game is used for adversarial training to harden agents against worst-case observation/dynamics shifts (the sim-to-real 'reality gap'), motivated by security-critical deployments -- autonomous driving, robotics sim-to-real, cyber-physical/network-security red-team-vs-blue-team settings, and smart-grid/industrial control (e.g., L2RPN grid controllers shown vulnerable in cited work).
limitations: As a survey it contributes no new algorithm and no empirical validation, so its comparative claims about which training strategies work best are inherited from cited works rather than independently measured. By design it scopes out reward, state-set, action-set, and observation-support alterations, and out-of-domain/support-shift issues, so its 'unified' framework covers only observation and dynamics perturbations. The formalization assumes a single training POMDP with the deployment environment distribution unknown at train time, and much of the tractability discussion (e.g., SA-rectangular uncertainty sets) applies to tabular/idealized settings. The computational-cost model (Eq. 28) is a first-order proxy that treats white-box forward passes and black-box queries as equivalent and rests on the rule-of-thumb c_bwd ~= 2 c_fwd, not on measured runtimes. Coverage is inevitably a snapshot and, being an arXiv v3 (June 2026), cites forward-dated works whose results the survey does not verify.

datasets_used: None of its own -- this is a survey with no original experiments.

Referenced (in cited works, not measured here): MuJoCo continuous-control benchmarks (basis of the RRLS robust-RL suite and cited co-training/ATLA results); Atari-style pixel observation tasks; the L2RPN power-grid operation benchmark (cited as a domain where RL controllers were shown vulnerable and adversarial training helped).
quantitative_results: No empirical metrics are produced by the survey. The only quantitative artifacts are analytical: a normalized computational-cost proxy S_total = T_exec*(N_fwd*c_fwd + N_bwd*c_bwd) + T_init*(N_fwd^init*c_fwd + N_bwd^init*c_bwd) (Eq. 28), with the stated rule-of-thumb c_bwd ~= 2*c_fwd (backprop roughly doubles a forward pass).

Structural/scale facts of the survey itself: organizes the field into a 9-section framework; taxonomy spans 5 perturbable elements (observation, action, current state, transition function, next state) and 2 altered POMDP components (O and T); catalogs dozens of named attacks (e.g., FGSM, BIM, PGD, JSMA, DeepFool, C&W, Square, NES, SPSA, HSJA, RARL, ATLA, PA-AD, PR-MDP, M2TD3) and training methods (ATLA, EPOpt, NFSP, PSRO, A2P-SAC, SC-RARL, WR2L, DRAGEN, UED/PAIRED, TC-RMDP, RAT-LD, TDRT, WocaR-RL) across Tables 1-3.
baselines_compared: None -- no head-to-head empirical comparison is run. The survey instead qualitatively contrasts attack FAMILIES by computational cost (single-step gradient vs. iterative white-box vs. query-based black-box vs. adversarial-policy vs. universal vs. sparsity/timing) and contrasts training STRATEGIES (constant vs. co-trained vs. mixture/population vs. worst-episode) and tooling (general-ML libraries CleverHans, ART, TorchAttacks, RobustBench, AttackBench vs. RL-specific suites RRLS and Robust-Gymnasium).
